item.content:encoded: Never thought this one would break +100!

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Ah yes, the Banhammer skip. I've got to potential links here. The first off is the mention of Site Director Gillespie. Roget did a great job building up this character in other works, but I would argue that the best piece featuring the character is I Hope That I Get Old Before I Die, which can be in-line linked from her mention in the article. Additionally, the mention of Multi-U can be linked to the Multi-U 101 orientation. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Further testing with D-class has been suspended to a maximum of 3 strikes. Site Director Gillespie.

To this:

Further testing with D-class has been suspended to a maximum of 3 strikes. Site Director Gillespie.

Changing this:

D-456692 was located by elements of Multi-U in designated alternate universe U-1135.

To this:

D-456692 was located by elements of Multi-U in designated alternate universe U-1135.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Foundation probes orbiting Neptune and its moons detected a number of corpses in the orbit of Triton shortly after discovery of these reports.

This is a fun mental image.

Article's not my thing though, "mysterious dollar-store product with contrastingly unsafe effects" and "thing with unpredictable effects paired with an experiment log ten miles long" are elements of Series I that I'd really like to stay there

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like to see what happens if a person hits themselves with one of the hammers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded. At some point I was sure Foundation scientists just like to hit people with hammers for shit and giggles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"SCP-3728's testing facility was destroyed in a concussive blast."

I was kinda meh about the intro but that line got my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
CAN'T TOUCH THIS

BIIIIIIIIIIYYYYYYNNNNGGGGGGGGG

(Class-D gets knocked into Jupiter's orbit and manages to meet The Destroyer)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it makes sense given where it is, since Director Gillespie is all about testing potentially useful anomalies. My only quibble with this is that there's some repetition in the main description, especially with every paragraph starting with "SCP-3728…" which could probably be corrected without too much difficulty. The containment procedures felt light and didn't really have any information not seen later in the article. I did like how it thought through a goofy anomaly thoroughly exploring the effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fair criticism. There are a number of reasons I could see the Foundation performing these tests, (weaponization, attempting to determine how some individuals survive hits while others do not, what happens after X number of strikes). The resources expended could be justified through the study of the way this defies physics, the potential benefits a device capable of generating a bubble like the one generated here might present for field work, etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The main issue I had was that the resources the foundation spent on testing these seem really excessive, especially for safe class objects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
interference from an additional body
if you assume point masses and zero drag

This is an important thing here: realistic orbital mechanics have to account for things like interference from additional bodies, non-point masses, and drag. The required trajectories and travel times are improbable to the point of almost certainly being a facet of the anomaly, but you can get things into all sorts of orbits without even needing fuel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show physics
- hide physics

Simplistically, gravity forms potential wells, where the potential energy close to the body is much lower than the potential energy far away. As you move towards the central body on a freefall trajectory (e.g. without firing a rocket engine, as I'm assuming these poor D-class are not able to do), you gain kinetic energy (speed) equal to the potential energy you lose, in accordance with conservation of energy. If you are released from rest at a certain height above a body, your total energy (assuming you don't actually strike the body) will stay the same, and you'll end up back at the altitude where you started. Stable orbits around a body can only exist for a certain range of total energies at a given altitude; if your energy is too large, you'll fly away no matter what, and if it's too small, you'll fall into the central body.
The result of this is that, if you come in from a large distance away from the body in question, your total energy (if not altered by drag, tidal forces, N-body effects, etc) will allow you to return to the height you started from, which is, in the case of these hammers, all the way back at Earth, and you won't get into a closed orbit. Since energy can't be created or destroyed, you have to find a way to get rid of it in order to get it down into the range where your orbit will be stable and prevent yourself from flying off again. Spacecraft do this either by firing rockets or by dipping low into the atmosphere and hoping their heatshields hold. D-class ejected from the Earth by surprise have no such options, so in a truly overwhelming majority of cases, they're going to end up crashed, burnt, or orbiting the sun - capturing into orbit of another solar system body is a very edge case.

Captured satellites are either the result of the non-spherical gravitation effects I mentioned before, or interference from an additional body. Tidal forces on large bodies tend to drag down their orbits at the periapsis and make them more circular, which can bring them into closed orbits if they pass the right way. It's not something that can happen at all if you assume point masses and large distances, so if you're talking about human bodies and high orbit around Jupiter, tidal capture simply isn't happening. Multi-body interference is more likely to work, but it's the sort of thing that will only happen with extremely narrow launch windows and extremely specific trajectories. The way the article is currently written, it sounds like "High-orbit around Jupiter" is assumed to just be some altitude away from Earth, which is wrong. As I said, I don't mind if being teleported or dragged into orbit of various solar system bodies is part of the anomaly, but I sure don't see that written in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fixed the wording of this issue

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't Site-77 be inside that area of 2 light years?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spacio-Temporal Absolute Location Key Reader (STALKER)

wow I think that was my favorite part of the whole skip, whoever made up that acronym must be a really cool dude

Seriously though, this is a good skip. Weird item, weird effect, but creative and fun while also horrifying. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sweeps (of Site-77 and an area of 2 blah blah) were unable to locate D-456692.

Maybe it could say

Even after sweeping Site-77 and an area of 2 light years in diameter, D-456692 has not been located.

Or something like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sweeps of Site-77 and an area of 2 lightyears in diameter, centered around the Earth, were unable to locate D-456692.

An area of 2 lightyears in diameter can't locate a D-class? I don't understand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you need to be able to exert another impulse to slow yourself down and get into a closed orbit, or you just end up flying off again.

Not necessarily. A lot of the moons the gas giants have are captured satellites- remember that gravity is acceleration; come in at the right angle and it can decelerate you into a clean orbit. Jupiter and Saturn in particular are like big trash dumps, they gather up all sorts of garbage from around the solar system this way.

Still odd that such a high percentage of them end up around Jupiter specifically, but I'm chalking that up as part of the anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Overall, I liked this one. I have one nitpick, though - an object catapulted away from Earth at escape speed would have a very poor chance of ending up in a stable orbit of anything besides the sun. This article references victims winding up in Jupiter orbit, which is highly unlikely, and also Triton orbit, which is practically impossible (and you mention that multiple subjects have ended up there. For one, both of these objects is really far away, and the likelihood of passing anywhere near them by accident would be absolutely vanishing. Two, you can't generally get into orbit around anything in space just by flying near it - tiny effects due to non-spherical gravitation nonwithstanding, you need to be able to exert another impulse to slow yourself down and get into a closed orbit, or you just end up flying off again. If you meant that this skip actually teleports you such that your position and velocity put you in a stable orbit of certain solar system bodies, fine, but I don't see that in the article - it sounds like you mean that they just flew out into space and happened to get captured by a passing moon of Neptune, which is so unlikely as to be unreasonable.

Other than that, I thought this was a very amusing and well-written article. +1, albeit with some reservation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like that the last guy literally got knocked into next week (And an alternate universe.)

Cool work. You did a lot with what should have been a very simple concept (tools that produce disproportionate results are not a new idea, of course.)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A reminder to all that I have dibs on writing U-1135

get hype

(also yay this was finally posted)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey folks, so here is a fun little SCP about children's toy hammers horrifically twisted into real life ban/repulsion hammers. Many thanks to Ninevolt for huge contributions to the log ideas, Roget, weryllium, Icepick, Tufto, Cyantreuse, LordStoneFish and a bunch of other people I'm probably forgetting.
